As the market continues to evolve, this selective approach may help investors navigate potential challenges while positioning for long-term growth. The current climate underscores the importance of careful research and professional advice when making property investment decisions.

item.title: Escalating service charges are biggest concern
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Rising service charges have emerged as a significant concern for homeowners and property investors across the UK, according to recent survey findings. With costs for property maintenance and management escalating, many are calling for greater transparency and more control over these expenses. This issue is particularly pressing for those living in leasehold properties, where service charges can form a substantial part of annual housing costs.

The impact of rising service charges

Service charges cover essential costs such as building maintenance, cleaning, insurance, and repairs in shared properties. However, many homeowners have reported sharp increases in these fees, often without clear justification. This trend can place additional financial strain on residents, particularly those on fixed incomes or with tight budgets. For property investors, escalating service charges can also affect rental yields and overall returns.

Some leaseholders have expressed frustration at the lack of control they have over these costs, as decisions about maintenance and service providers are typically made by freeholders or managing agents. Without proper oversight, there is a risk that charges could become disproportionate to the services provided.

Calling for greater transparency

The survey findings have prompted calls for improved transparency and accountability in how service charges are calculated and managed. Homeowners and industry experts alike are advocating for clearer communication from managing agents and freeholders, ensuring that residents understand what they are paying for and why costs may be rising.

  • Detailed breakdowns of service charge costs.
  • Regular updates on planned maintenance works.
  • Opportunities for leaseholders to scrutinise and challenge charges.

Some suggest that regulatory changes may be needed to empower leaseholders and ensure fairness in the way service charges are administered. This could include stricter guidelines for managing agents and greater rights for homeowners to influence decisions about property management.

As service charges continue to climb, homeowners and investors may wish to thoroughly review their costs and seek advice if they believe charges are excessive. Greater transparency and control over these expenses could help alleviate some of the financial pressures faced by residents and ensure that property management remains fair and affordable.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Rising service charges have emerged as a significant concern for homeowners and property investors across the UK, according to recent survey findings. With costs for property maintenance and management escalating, many are calling for greater transparency and more control over these expenses. This issue is particularly pressing for those living in leasehold properties, where service charges can form a substantial part of annual housing costs.

The impact of rising service charges

Service charges cover essential costs such as building maintenance, cleaning, insurance, and repairs in shared properties. However, many homeowners have reported sharp increases in these fees, often without clear justification. This trend can place additional financial strain on residents, particularly those on fixed incomes or with tight budgets. For property investors, escalating service charges can also affect rental yields and overall returns.

Some leaseholders have expressed frustration at the lack of control they have over these costs, as decisions about maintenance and service providers are typically made by freeholders or managing agents. Without proper oversight, there is a risk that charges could become disproportionate to the services provided.

Calling for greater transparency

The survey findings have prompted calls for improved transparency and accountability in how service charges are calculated and managed. Homeowners and industry experts alike are advocating for clearer communication from managing agents and freeholders, ensuring that residents understand what they are paying for and why costs may be rising.

  • Detailed breakdowns of service charge costs.
  • Regular updates on planned maintenance works.
  • Opportunities for leaseholders to scrutinise and challenge charges.

Some suggest that regulatory changes may be needed to empower leaseholders and ensure fairness in the way service charges are administered. This could include stricter guidelines for managing agents and greater rights for homeowners to influence decisions about property management.

As service charges continue to climb, homeowners and investors may wish to thoroughly review their costs and seek advice if they believe charges are excessive. Greater transparency and control over these expenses could help alleviate some of the financial pressures faced by residents and ensure that property management remains fair and affordable.

item.title: 300 lots in July resi auction
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property investors and homebuyers looking for opportunities across the UK market may find interest in Allsop's upcoming residential auction this July. The two-day event will feature approximately 300 properties, offering a diverse selection of lots spanning London and regional markets.

Wide-ranging property selection

The auction presents a varied portfolio of residential properties, catering to different investment strategies and buyer requirements. From London postcodes to regional locations, the catalogue includes:

  • Residential investment properties with existing tenancies
  • Vacant homes suitable for refurbishment
  • Development opportunities with planning potential
  • Mixed-use properties in urban and suburban locations

Considerations for auction buyers

For those considering property auctions as a route to purchase, there are several factors to bear in mind. Auction purchases typically require:

  • A 10% deposit payable immediately after the hammer falls
  • Completion within 28 days (unless otherwise specified)
  • Thorough due diligence before bidding
  • Arranged finance in place prior to the auction date

Prospective buyers might wish to consult with a mortgage broker specialising in auction finance, as traditional mortgage applications may not align with auction timescales.

With properties across multiple price points and locations, Allsop's July auction could present opportunities for both seasoned investors and first-time buyers willing to navigate the auction process. As with any property purchase, conducting proper research and seeking professional advice remains crucial.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property investors and homebuyers looking for opportunities across the UK market may find interest in Allsop's upcoming residential auction this July. The two-day event will feature approximately 300 properties, offering a diverse selection of lots spanning London and regional markets.

Wide-ranging property selection

The auction presents a varied portfolio of residential properties, catering to different investment strategies and buyer requirements. From London postcodes to regional locations, the catalogue includes:

  • Residential investment properties with existing tenancies
  • Vacant homes suitable for refurbishment
  • Development opportunities with planning potential
  • Mixed-use properties in urban and suburban locations

Considerations for auction buyers

For those considering property auctions as a route to purchase, there are several factors to bear in mind. Auction purchases typically require:

  • A 10% deposit payable immediately after the hammer falls
  • Completion within 28 days (unless otherwise specified)
  • Thorough due diligence before bidding
  • Arranged finance in place prior to the auction date

Prospective buyers might wish to consult with a mortgage broker specialising in auction finance, as traditional mortgage applications may not align with auction timescales.

With properties across multiple price points and locations, Allsop's July auction could present opportunities for both seasoned investors and first-time buyers willing to navigate the auction process. As with any property purchase, conducting proper research and seeking professional advice remains crucial.

item.title: Property fraud: the free protection every landlord should use
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property fraud remains an underappreciated risk for homeowners and landlords alike. Whilst relatively uncommon, these crimes can lead to devastating financial consequences and lengthy legal battles when they occur. Two high-profile cases highlight how easily properties can be targeted – and why preventative measures are essential.

When property fraud strikes

In one notable case, a homeowner named Mr Guy discovered his property had been fraudulently transferred to a company without his knowledge. The new "owners" then secured substantial borrowing from Barclays Bank against the property. Despite Mr Guy's legal challenge, the court ruled the bank had acted in good faith, leaving him with significant losses.

Another distressing case involved Reverend Mike Hall, who found his Luton home had been sold by fraudsters while he worked in Wales. The vicar only learned of the crime when neighbours reported suspicious activity. Though his name was eventually restored to the Land Registry, it took nearly four years to regain possession from occupants who believed they had legitimate tenancy agreements.

Who's most vulnerable to property fraud?

Certain property owners face higher risks of becoming fraud victims:

  • Landlords with rental properties
  • Owners living overseas
  • Those with empty properties
  • Homeowners without mortgages

The common factor in these scenarios is often reduced oversight of the property. Fraudsters frequently target homes where owners might not immediately notice suspicious activity.

Protecting your property

HM Land Registry offers free protective measures that could help prevent such frauds. Property owners might consider:

  • Ensuring contact details with the Land Registry are current
  • Setting up property alerts to monitor for suspicious changes
  • Restricting certain transactions without additional verification

These simple steps could provide crucial early warnings if someone attempts to interfere with a property's ownership. With fraudsters becoming increasingly sophisticated, preventative measures offer valuable peace of mind for homeowners and landlords alike.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property fraud remains an underappreciated risk for homeowners and landlords alike. Whilst relatively uncommon, these crimes can lead to devastating financial consequences and lengthy legal battles when they occur. Two high-profile cases highlight how easily properties can be targeted – and why preventative measures are essential.

When property fraud strikes

In one notable case, a homeowner named Mr Guy discovered his property had been fraudulently transferred to a company without his knowledge. The new "owners" then secured substantial borrowing from Barclays Bank against the property. Despite Mr Guy's legal challenge, the court ruled the bank had acted in good faith, leaving him with significant losses.

Another distressing case involved Reverend Mike Hall, who found his Luton home had been sold by fraudsters while he worked in Wales. The vicar only learned of the crime when neighbours reported suspicious activity. Though his name was eventually restored to the Land Registry, it took nearly four years to regain possession from occupants who believed they had legitimate tenancy agreements.

Who's most vulnerable to property fraud?

Certain property owners face higher risks of becoming fraud victims:

  • Landlords with rental properties
  • Owners living overseas
  • Those with empty properties
  • Homeowners without mortgages

The common factor in these scenarios is often reduced oversight of the property. Fraudsters frequently target homes where owners might not immediately notice suspicious activity.

Protecting your property

HM Land Registry offers free protective measures that could help prevent such frauds. Property owners might consider:

  • Ensuring contact details with the Land Registry are current
  • Setting up property alerts to monitor for suspicious changes
  • Restricting certain transactions without additional verification

These simple steps could provide crucial early warnings if someone attempts to interfere with a property's ownership. With fraudsters becoming increasingly sophisticated, preventative measures offer valuable peace of mind for homeowners and landlords alike.

item.title: Industry reaction: all eyes on interest rates as pricing levels dip in July
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK property asking prices fell by an unusually steep 1% (£3,832) in July, settling at £372,359 according to Rightmove's latest index. This drop significantly exceeds the typical 0.2% seasonal decline, with estate agents pointing to summer distractions including major sporting events, warm weather and political uncertainty as contributing factors. The market currently offers buyers an abundance of choice, with property supply nearing a 12-year seasonal high.

Mortgage rates and market confidence

Sales activity during the first half of 2026 showed a 6% year-on-year decline, though remained level with 2024 figures. Industry experts suggest that elevated mortgage rates following geopolitical tensions have placed additional pressure on the housing market. Nathan Emerson, Propertymark's chief executive, noted: "While the year began with optimism, global uncertainties have since taken centre stage. Many households are understandably prioritising financial caution to protect their budgets."

Emerson added: "The Bank of England's upcoming base rate decision will be crucial in shaping market sentiment, particularly for prospective home movers and those with tracker mortgages." Mortgage expert Matt Smith from Rightmove observed that although current rates remain higher than many borrowers anticipated at the start of the year, lenders continue to demonstrate strong appetite, maintaining competitive market conditions.

Seller strategies in a shifting market

Estate agents report that accurate pricing has become increasingly vital for sellers hoping to secure buyers in the current climate. Chris Thomas of Wiglesworth & Co Estate Agents commented: "Despite being more challenging than last year, the market has proven resilient considering the mortgage rate increases we've seen. In these conditions, correct initial pricing makes all the difference for sellers."

Industry analysts suggest that whilst recent minor reductions in mortgage pricing have provided some relief, these could prove temporary. Smith noted: "Should we see sustained reductions in mortgage rates, this would undoubtedly boost both confidence and affordability across the market." However, experts caution that the housing sector remains sensitive to both domestic economic policy and international events.

As the summer progresses, potential buyers and sellers may wish to monitor both interest rate decisions and lender pricing closely, whilst remaining mindful that seasonal factors traditionally lead to quieter market conditions during these months.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK property asking prices fell by an unusually steep 1% (£3,832) in July, settling at £372,359 according to Rightmove's latest index. This drop significantly exceeds the typical 0.2% seasonal decline, with estate agents pointing to summer distractions including major sporting events, warm weather and political uncertainty as contributing factors. The market currently offers buyers an abundance of choice, with property supply nearing a 12-year seasonal high.

Mortgage rates and market confidence

Sales activity during the first half of 2026 showed a 6% year-on-year decline, though remained level with 2024 figures. Industry experts suggest that elevated mortgage rates following geopolitical tensions have placed additional pressure on the housing market. Nathan Emerson, Propertymark's chief executive, noted: "While the year began with optimism, global uncertainties have since taken centre stage. Many households are understandably prioritising financial caution to protect their budgets."

Emerson added: "The Bank of England's upcoming base rate decision will be crucial in shaping market sentiment, particularly for prospective home movers and those with tracker mortgages." Mortgage expert Matt Smith from Rightmove observed that although current rates remain higher than many borrowers anticipated at the start of the year, lenders continue to demonstrate strong appetite, maintaining competitive market conditions.

Seller strategies in a shifting market

Estate agents report that accurate pricing has become increasingly vital for sellers hoping to secure buyers in the current climate. Chris Thomas of Wiglesworth & Co Estate Agents commented: "Despite being more challenging than last year, the market has proven resilient considering the mortgage rate increases we've seen. In these conditions, correct initial pricing makes all the difference for sellers."

Industry analysts suggest that whilst recent minor reductions in mortgage pricing have provided some relief, these could prove temporary. Smith noted: "Should we see sustained reductions in mortgage rates, this would undoubtedly boost both confidence and affordability across the market." However, experts caution that the housing sector remains sensitive to both domestic economic policy and international events.

As the summer progresses, potential buyers and sellers may wish to monitor both interest rate decisions and lender pricing closely, whilst remaining mindful that seasonal factors traditionally lead to quieter market conditions during these months.

item.title: Renters’ Rights Act has made selling ‘too much of a risk’ for landlords
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act has prompted significant changes in the property market, with landlords now facing increased risks when selling rental properties. Recent data from Hamptons revealed that landlord purchases surpassed sales for the first time since 2019, with landlords accounting for 10.2% of property purchases in June, compared to 9.2% of homes listed for sale that were previously rented. This shift has been attributed to new regulations that have altered the landscape for property investors.

The Impact of the 12-Month Re-letting Ban

One of the most contentious aspects of the Renters’ Rights Act is the 12-month ban on re-letting a property after using a Ground 1A notice to evict a tenant for the purpose of selling. According to Mark Dawson, managing director of landlord adviser and eviction specialist AST Assistance, this rule has removed a crucial safety net for landlords. In the past, landlords could re-let their property if a sale fell through, but this is no longer an option under the new legislation.

Dawson highlighted that more than half (51%) of rental properties failed to complete sales in 2025, leaving landlords vulnerable to significant financial strain. If a sale collapses, landlords may face a situation where their property remains empty for 12 months, resulting in lost rental income and ongoing costs such as mortgage payments, council tax, and utility bills. This has led many landlords to view selling as “too much of a risk” under the current rules.

Strategies for Landlords Considering a Sale

For landlords who still wish to sell, Dawson emphasised the importance of having a solid plan in place. This includes identifying buyers who are less likely to withdraw from the transaction, such as those who have already had an offer accepted on their own property or first-time buyers with a mortgage agreement in principle. These buyers may be more committed to completing the purchase, reducing the likelihood of delays or cancellations.

However, Dawson also raised concerns about the potential unintended consequences of the Renters’ Rights Act. If landlords delay both selling and re-letting to avoid the 12-month penalty, properties may remain vacant on both sides of the market. This could exacerbate housing shortages, ultimately disadvantaging both buyers and renters – the very groups the legislation was designed to protect.

The Broader Implications for the Private Rented Sector

The changes brought about by the Renters’ Rights Act have sparked debate about their long-term impact on the private rented sector. While the legislation aims to provide greater security for tenants, it may inadvertently discourage landlords from investing in or maintaining rental properties. This could lead to a reduction in the availability of rental homes, further tightening the market and potentially driving up rents.

As the property market continues to adapt to these new regulations, landlords might consider seeking professional advice to navigate the complexities of selling rental properties. Understanding the risks and planning carefully could help mitigate some of the challenges posed by the current rules.

The Renters’ Rights Act represents a significant shift in the balance between tenant protections and landlord flexibility. While its intentions are commendable, its practical implications for the property market remain to be seen. Landlords and tenants alike may need to adjust their strategies in response to these evolving dynamics.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act has prompted significant changes in the property market, with landlords now facing increased risks when selling rental properties. Recent data from Hamptons revealed that landlord purchases surpassed sales for the first time since 2019, with landlords accounting for 10.2% of property purchases in June, compared to 9.2% of homes listed for sale that were previously rented. This shift has been attributed to new regulations that have altered the landscape for property investors.

The Impact of the 12-Month Re-letting Ban

One of the most contentious aspects of the Renters’ Rights Act is the 12-month ban on re-letting a property after using a Ground 1A notice to evict a tenant for the purpose of selling. According to Mark Dawson, managing director of landlord adviser and eviction specialist AST Assistance, this rule has removed a crucial safety net for landlords. In the past, landlords could re-let their property if a sale fell through, but this is no longer an option under the new legislation.

Dawson highlighted that more than half (51%) of rental properties failed to complete sales in 2025, leaving landlords vulnerable to significant financial strain. If a sale collapses, landlords may face a situation where their property remains empty for 12 months, resulting in lost rental income and ongoing costs such as mortgage payments, council tax, and utility bills. This has led many landlords to view selling as “too much of a risk” under the current rules.

Strategies for Landlords Considering a Sale

For landlords who still wish to sell, Dawson emphasised the importance of having a solid plan in place. This includes identifying buyers who are less likely to withdraw from the transaction, such as those who have already had an offer accepted on their own property or first-time buyers with a mortgage agreement in principle. These buyers may be more committed to completing the purchase, reducing the likelihood of delays or cancellations.

However, Dawson also raised concerns about the potential unintended consequences of the Renters’ Rights Act. If landlords delay both selling and re-letting to avoid the 12-month penalty, properties may remain vacant on both sides of the market. This could exacerbate housing shortages, ultimately disadvantaging both buyers and renters – the very groups the legislation was designed to protect.

The Broader Implications for the Private Rented Sector

The changes brought about by the Renters’ Rights Act have sparked debate about their long-term impact on the private rented sector. While the legislation aims to provide greater security for tenants, it may inadvertently discourage landlords from investing in or maintaining rental properties. This could lead to a reduction in the availability of rental homes, further tightening the market and potentially driving up rents.

As the property market continues to adapt to these new regulations, landlords might consider seeking professional advice to navigate the complexities of selling rental properties. Understanding the risks and planning carefully could help mitigate some of the challenges posed by the current rules.

The Renters’ Rights Act represents a significant shift in the balance between tenant protections and landlord flexibility. While its intentions are commendable, its practical implications for the property market remain to be seen. Landlords and tenants alike may need to adjust their strategies in response to these evolving dynamics.

item.title: Property auction activity jumps by more than a third in June
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property auction market experienced a significant surge in activity during June, with the number of lots offered rising by over a third compared to the same period last year. According to data from the Essential Information Group (EIG), 4,008 lots were made available, marking a 35.3% increase. Sales volumes also grew by 31.3%, while the total funds raised climbed by 33.5% to £519.7 million. Despite this growth, the national success rate dipped slightly to 65.3%, as the increase in available properties outpaced buyer demand.

Strong Performance Across Residential and Commercial Sectors

Both residential and commercial properties saw robust year-on-year growth in June. The number of lots offered, sales completed, and funds raised all increased significantly across these sectors. This suggests that auctions remain an attractive option for both homeowners and investors, particularly amidst ongoing economic uncertainty. Regional performance was also noteworthy, with the South West and Yorkshire and the Humber emerging as particularly strong performers during the April to June period. These areas recorded substantial increases in activity and sales, contributing to the overall growth of the auction market.

London also posted impressive results, with the number of lots sold and the total value raised both rising by more than 20% year on year. Scotland, meanwhile, saw another sharp increase in auction activity, although its success rates remained below the national average. This regional variation highlights the diverse dynamics shaping the UK property market, with certain areas experiencing stronger demand than others.

Factors Driving Auction Market Growth

The rise in properties coming to auction may partly reflect landlords reassessing their portfolios following the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act in May. This legislative change has prompted some property owners to explore alternative strategies, including selling through auctions. Additionally, sustained buyer demand and a steady flow of stock have helped maintain healthy levels of activity in the market. Stuart Collar-Brown, president of NAVA Propertymark, noted that auctions continue to provide certainty for both buyers and sellers in an uncertain economic climate.

Collar-Brown also highlighted that legislative changes in the property sector may be influencing commercial sales volumes and values. Investors appear to favour longer-term leases over the now-defunct assured shorthold tenancy model in the residential market. This shift could be driving increased interest in commercial property auctions. He emphasised the importance of realistic pricing in creating a competitive bidding environment, which has contributed to the sector’s ongoing growth.

As the UK auction market continues to expand, homeowners and investors might consider the potential benefits of this sales method. With strong regional performance and sustained demand, auctions remain a viable option for those seeking certainty in an unpredictable property landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property auction market experienced a significant surge in activity during June, with the number of lots offered rising by over a third compared to the same period last year. According to data from the Essential Information Group (EIG), 4,008 lots were made available, marking a 35.3% increase. Sales volumes also grew by 31.3%, while the total funds raised climbed by 33.5% to £519.7 million. Despite this growth, the national success rate dipped slightly to 65.3%, as the increase in available properties outpaced buyer demand.

Strong Performance Across Residential and Commercial Sectors

Both residential and commercial properties saw robust year-on-year growth in June. The number of lots offered, sales completed, and funds raised all increased significantly across these sectors. This suggests that auctions remain an attractive option for both homeowners and investors, particularly amidst ongoing economic uncertainty. Regional performance was also noteworthy, with the South West and Yorkshire and the Humber emerging as particularly strong performers during the April to June period. These areas recorded substantial increases in activity and sales, contributing to the overall growth of the auction market.

London also posted impressive results, with the number of lots sold and the total value raised both rising by more than 20% year on year. Scotland, meanwhile, saw another sharp increase in auction activity, although its success rates remained below the national average. This regional variation highlights the diverse dynamics shaping the UK property market, with certain areas experiencing stronger demand than others.

Factors Driving Auction Market Growth

The rise in properties coming to auction may partly reflect landlords reassessing their portfolios following the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act in May. This legislative change has prompted some property owners to explore alternative strategies, including selling through auctions. Additionally, sustained buyer demand and a steady flow of stock have helped maintain healthy levels of activity in the market. Stuart Collar-Brown, president of NAVA Propertymark, noted that auctions continue to provide certainty for both buyers and sellers in an uncertain economic climate.

Collar-Brown also highlighted that legislative changes in the property sector may be influencing commercial sales volumes and values. Investors appear to favour longer-term leases over the now-defunct assured shorthold tenancy model in the residential market. This shift could be driving increased interest in commercial property auctions. He emphasised the importance of realistic pricing in creating a competitive bidding environment, which has contributed to the sector’s ongoing growth.

As the UK auction market continues to expand, homeowners and investors might consider the potential benefits of this sales method. With strong regional performance and sustained demand, auctions remain a viable option for those seeking certainty in an unpredictable property landscape.

item.title: Scottish housing market slows as sales and price growth lose momentum
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Scotland's property market has shown signs of cooling, with recent data revealing slower growth in sales, prices and new listings amidst rising mortgage costs. The latest figures suggest this trend may continue in the coming months, creating a more subdued housing landscape compared to recent years.

Market momentum eases across key indicators

The Scottish Government's Housing Market Review highlights a gradual deceleration in market activity during 2025-26. While annual property sales increased by 4.5% to 104,339 transactions, growth rates tapered off significantly towards the end of the period - dropping from 6.1% in Q3 2025 to just 3.3% by Q1 2026. More recent Land and Buildings Transaction Tax returns paint a similar picture, showing year-on-year declines of 2.1% in April and 3.9% in May.

Price growth has followed this pattern, with the UK House Price Index recording a 3.3% annual increase in Scottish property values - below the long-term average of 2.7%. The slowdown became particularly noticeable in early 2026, when quarterly growth fell to 2%, the weakest performance since mid-2024. Edinburgh's market appears particularly affected, with new listings dropping 8.9% during Q2 2026.

Mortgage rates and housing supply factors

The cooling market coincides with rising borrowing costs, with average two-year fixed rates climbing nearly a full percentage point between January and May 2026. Products for buyers with 25% deposits increased from 4% to 4.9%, while 90% loan-to-value mortgages rose from 4.3% to 5.3%. Despite these increases, first-time buyer mortgage approvals grew by 6%, outpacing the 4.4% rise in home mover lending.

Scotland's rental market also showed unusual movement, with newly let properties seeing a 0.4% price drop in Q1 2026 - the first decline in nearly a decade. When adjusted for inflation, this represents a more substantial 3.4% decrease, continuing a three-quarter trend of real-terms rental price falls.

The supply side presents further challenges, with new-build completions falling 10% to 17,268 in the year to March 2026. Starts declined by 4.4%, suggesting the pipeline may remain constrained. Affordable housing completions through government programmes also dropped by 8%, though increased starts and approvals could signal future improvement in this sector.

Prospective buyers and sellers in Scotland may wish to consider these shifting market conditions carefully, particularly the interplay between mortgage costs, supply levels and pricing trends. With multiple indicators pointing towards cooling activity, the coming months could present different opportunities compared to recent years.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Scotland's property market has shown signs of cooling, with recent data revealing slower growth in sales, prices and new listings amidst rising mortgage costs. The latest figures suggest this trend may continue in the coming months, creating a more subdued housing landscape compared to recent years.

Market momentum eases across key indicators

The Scottish Government's Housing Market Review highlights a gradual deceleration in market activity during 2025-26. While annual property sales increased by 4.5% to 104,339 transactions, growth rates tapered off significantly towards the end of the period - dropping from 6.1% in Q3 2025 to just 3.3% by Q1 2026. More recent Land and Buildings Transaction Tax returns paint a similar picture, showing year-on-year declines of 2.1% in April and 3.9% in May.

Price growth has followed this pattern, with the UK House Price Index recording a 3.3% annual increase in Scottish property values - below the long-term average of 2.7%. The slowdown became particularly noticeable in early 2026, when quarterly growth fell to 2%, the weakest performance since mid-2024. Edinburgh's market appears particularly affected, with new listings dropping 8.9% during Q2 2026.

Mortgage rates and housing supply factors

The cooling market coincides with rising borrowing costs, with average two-year fixed rates climbing nearly a full percentage point between January and May 2026. Products for buyers with 25% deposits increased from 4% to 4.9%, while 90% loan-to-value mortgages rose from 4.3% to 5.3%. Despite these increases, first-time buyer mortgage approvals grew by 6%, outpacing the 4.4% rise in home mover lending.

Scotland's rental market also showed unusual movement, with newly let properties seeing a 0.4% price drop in Q1 2026 - the first decline in nearly a decade. When adjusted for inflation, this represents a more substantial 3.4% decrease, continuing a three-quarter trend of real-terms rental price falls.

The supply side presents further challenges, with new-build completions falling 10% to 17,268 in the year to March 2026. Starts declined by 4.4%, suggesting the pipeline may remain constrained. Affordable housing completions through government programmes also dropped by 8%, though increased starts and approvals could signal future improvement in this sector.

Prospective buyers and sellers in Scotland may wish to consider these shifting market conditions carefully, particularly the interplay between mortgage costs, supply levels and pricing trends. With multiple indicators pointing towards cooling activity, the coming months could present different opportunities compared to recent years.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #445
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent developments in the UK property market highlight shifting dynamics for both landlords and tenants, from rising court costs to changing renter demographics. With affordability pressures growing and housing supply fluctuations, stakeholders across the sector may need to reassess their positions.

Court fee increases add to landlord pressures

Landlords face higher costs when pursuing possession claims, with government adjustments linking court fees to inflation. The price for possession claims rises from £404 to £415, while bailiff fees increase from £148 to £152. These changes come amidst ongoing criticism of court backlogs, with some landlords reportedly waiting months for possession cases to progress.

Tenants challenging rent increases won’t see equivalent rises, with their application fees remaining at £47. Property professionals have noted the strain these delays place on landlords, particularly those with problem tenants or mortgage repayments to meet.

Rental market shows signs of rebalancing

Recent data suggests the rental market may be stabilising after periods of extreme competition. Available properties remain 1% lower than last year, but enquiry rates have dropped from 11 to 10 per listing compared to 2023 – a significant decrease from 22 enquiries per property in 2022. Some analysts report rental supply reaching seven-year highs, with projections of a 17% increase by 2026.

Rental growth continues across the UK, with average prices outside London increasing by 2.3% annually, while the capital sees a more modest 2% rise. Affordability concerns persist as the primary factor influencing tenant decisions, particularly given wider cost-of-living pressures.

Pensioner renters pose new challenges

Research from the Association of British Insurers suggests a dramatic shift in renter demographics, predicting one in three renting households will be pensioners by 2044. This could see two million additional retirees relying on the private rental sector, raising questions about housing security and financial stability in later life.

Experts highlight particular concerns for those without substantial pensions or investments, where rental costs could significantly deplete retirement savings. Landlords may also need to adapt their approach as the tenant population ages, including preparing for sensitive situations like tenancies continuing after a tenant’s death.

As the property market continues to evolve, both landlords and tenants may wish to stay informed about these developing trends. From legal costs to demographic shifts, understanding these changes could help stakeholders make more informed decisions about their housing arrangements.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent developments in the UK property market highlight shifting dynamics for both landlords and tenants, from rising court costs to changing renter demographics. With affordability pressures growing and housing supply fluctuations, stakeholders across the sector may need to reassess their positions.

Court fee increases add to landlord pressures

Landlords face higher costs when pursuing possession claims, with government adjustments linking court fees to inflation. The price for possession claims rises from £404 to £415, while bailiff fees increase from £148 to £152. These changes come amidst ongoing criticism of court backlogs, with some landlords reportedly waiting months for possession cases to progress.

Tenants challenging rent increases won’t see equivalent rises, with their application fees remaining at £47. Property professionals have noted the strain these delays place on landlords, particularly those with problem tenants or mortgage repayments to meet.

Rental market shows signs of rebalancing

Recent data suggests the rental market may be stabilising after periods of extreme competition. Available properties remain 1% lower than last year, but enquiry rates have dropped from 11 to 10 per listing compared to 2023 – a significant decrease from 22 enquiries per property in 2022. Some analysts report rental supply reaching seven-year highs, with projections of a 17% increase by 2026.

Rental growth continues across the UK, with average prices outside London increasing by 2.3% annually, while the capital sees a more modest 2% rise. Affordability concerns persist as the primary factor influencing tenant decisions, particularly given wider cost-of-living pressures.

Pensioner renters pose new challenges

Research from the Association of British Insurers suggests a dramatic shift in renter demographics, predicting one in three renting households will be pensioners by 2044. This could see two million additional retirees relying on the private rental sector, raising questions about housing security and financial stability in later life.

Experts highlight particular concerns for those without substantial pensions or investments, where rental costs could significantly deplete retirement savings. Landlords may also need to adapt their approach as the tenant population ages, including preparing for sensitive situations like tenancies continuing after a tenant’s death.

As the property market continues to evolve, both landlords and tenants may wish to stay informed about these developing trends. From legal costs to demographic shifts, understanding these changes could help stakeholders make more informed decisions about their housing arrangements.

item.title: Estate agency warns Burnham over housing tax plans
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

A leading Scottish estate agency has cautioned against potential housing tax reforms under consideration by incoming prime minister Andy Burnham, warning that such measures could destabilise the property market. With reports suggesting the government may look to property wealth to fund new spending commitments, industry experts highlight the risks of policies targeting higher-value homes, landlord income and property assets.

Concerns over proposed property taxation

DJ Alexander Scotland has expressed particular concern about several tax proposals reportedly being explored, including:

  • Expanding taxes on high-value residential properties
  • Applying National Insurance to landlords' rental income
  • Broadening capital gains and inheritance tax rules
  • Introducing new wealth taxes targeting property assets

The agency argues these measures could disproportionately affect homeowners who have seen property values rise over time but may not have significant liquid assets. David Alexander, CEO of DJ Alexander Scotland, noted that many elderly homeowners in particular could face financial strain from annual levies based on property values rather than actual income.

Potential market impacts

The estate agency warns that such tax changes often fail to generate projected revenues while creating unintended consequences. Historical patterns suggest wealthy individuals frequently adjust their financial arrangements to mitigate tax impacts, potentially leaving middle-income homeowners bearing the brunt of new levies.

Alexander pointed to the significant inflation in house prices over recent decades - with average UK property values increasing approximately 800% since the 1980s compared to 207% for general inflation. This growth means many homeowners now live in properties worth substantially more than their purchase price, despite potentially having modest incomes.

The firm also cautioned that taxing property wealth could discourage investment in the housing sector at a time when the market faces multiple challenges. Landlords might reconsider rental property ownership if faced with additional taxation, potentially reducing available housing stock.

As the new government prepares its policy agenda, industry observers suggest alternative approaches to housing market reform that balance revenue needs with market stability. With many homeowners potentially affected by tax changes, the coming months may prove crucial for the UK property sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

A leading Scottish estate agency has cautioned against potential housing tax reforms under consideration by incoming prime minister Andy Burnham, warning that such measures could destabilise the property market. With reports suggesting the government may look to property wealth to fund new spending commitments, industry experts highlight the risks of policies targeting higher-value homes, landlord income and property assets.

Concerns over proposed property taxation

DJ Alexander Scotland has expressed particular concern about several tax proposals reportedly being explored, including:

  • Expanding taxes on high-value residential properties
  • Applying National Insurance to landlords' rental income
  • Broadening capital gains and inheritance tax rules
  • Introducing new wealth taxes targeting property assets

The agency argues these measures could disproportionately affect homeowners who have seen property values rise over time but may not have significant liquid assets. David Alexander, CEO of DJ Alexander Scotland, noted that many elderly homeowners in particular could face financial strain from annual levies based on property values rather than actual income.

Potential market impacts

The estate agency warns that such tax changes often fail to generate projected revenues while creating unintended consequences. Historical patterns suggest wealthy individuals frequently adjust their financial arrangements to mitigate tax impacts, potentially leaving middle-income homeowners bearing the brunt of new levies.

Alexander pointed to the significant inflation in house prices over recent decades - with average UK property values increasing approximately 800% since the 1980s compared to 207% for general inflation. This growth means many homeowners now live in properties worth substantially more than their purchase price, despite potentially having modest incomes.

The firm also cautioned that taxing property wealth could discourage investment in the housing sector at a time when the market faces multiple challenges. Landlords might reconsider rental property ownership if faced with additional taxation, potentially reducing available housing stock.

As the new government prepares its policy agenda, industry observers suggest alternative approaches to housing market reform that balance revenue needs with market stability. With many homeowners potentially affected by tax changes, the coming months may prove crucial for the UK property sector.

item.title: Foxtons shares fall sharply after £3m Renters’ Rights Act hit
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Foxtons Group, the London-focused estate agency, has seen its share price drop significantly following a £3 million earnings hit linked to the Renters’ (Reform) Act. The legislation, which introduced greater flexibility for tenants to end agreements, has contributed to a sharp rise in tenancy terminations, affecting the company’s short-term profitability.

Financial impact and market challenges

The estate agent reported an expected adjusted operating profit of £8.5 million for the first half of 2024 – down from £12.3 million during the same period last year. Alongside the rental market changes, Foxtons cited subdued residential sales activity, attributing this to political uncertainty, geopolitical tensions and elevated mortgage rates. The company’s shares fell nearly 11% in a single day, closing at 39p – roughly half their value at the beginning of the year.

Strategic response and future outlook

In response to these challenges, Foxtons has implemented cost-cutting measures designed to save £4.5 million annually. These include operational efficiencies and the relocation of its central London headquarters. Whilst acknowledging the current pressures, the agency expressed confidence in the long-term demand for professional lettings services under the new regulatory framework.

The company has revised its full-year adjusted operating profit forecast to between £17 million and £19 million, anticipating improved performance in the second half as seasonal lettings activity increases and tenancy termination rates stabilise. Property professionals will be watching closely to see how Foxtons adapts to these changing market conditions whilst maintaining its position in the competitive London property sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Foxtons Group, the London-focused estate agency, has seen its share price drop significantly following a £3 million earnings hit linked to the Renters’ (Reform) Act. The legislation, which introduced greater flexibility for tenants to end agreements, has contributed to a sharp rise in tenancy terminations, affecting the company’s short-term profitability.

Financial impact and market challenges

The estate agent reported an expected adjusted operating profit of £8.5 million for the first half of 2024 – down from £12.3 million during the same period last year. Alongside the rental market changes, Foxtons cited subdued residential sales activity, attributing this to political uncertainty, geopolitical tensions and elevated mortgage rates. The company’s shares fell nearly 11% in a single day, closing at 39p – roughly half their value at the beginning of the year.

Strategic response and future outlook

In response to these challenges, Foxtons has implemented cost-cutting measures designed to save £4.5 million annually. These include operational efficiencies and the relocation of its central London headquarters. Whilst acknowledging the current pressures, the agency expressed confidence in the long-term demand for professional lettings services under the new regulatory framework.

The company has revised its full-year adjusted operating profit forecast to between £17 million and £19 million, anticipating improved performance in the second half as seasonal lettings activity increases and tenancy termination rates stabilise. Property professionals will be watching closely to see how Foxtons adapts to these changing market conditions whilst maintaining its position in the competitive London property sector.

item.title: Millions in landlord fines go unpaid
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Local authorities across England are ramping up inspections of privately rented properties, yet a significant portion of fines issued to rogue landlords remains unpaid. According to data obtained by the National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA), councils collected only around 25% of the penalties imposed on landlords for housing offences, despite increased enforcement efforts.

Rising Inspections, Unpaid Fines

The NRLA’s Freedom of Information request revealed that inspections under the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) rose from 85,326 in 2021/22 to 91,620 in 2023/24. However, whilst inspections have increased, councils have struggled to recover fines. Between 2023/24 and 2024/25, English councils issued nearly £30 million in penalties but recouped just £7.5 million. This disparity highlights a growing challenge in ensuring that landlords who violate housing standards are held accountable.

Calls for Greater Transparency and Enforcement

The NRLA has urged councils to publish annual reports detailing their enforcement activities in the private rented sector. This call was echoed by the cross-party Housing Select Committee, which recently recommended stronger oversight of local authority enforcement. Ben Beadle, Chief Executive of the NRLA, emphasised the need for transparency, stating: “Councils must publish annual reports ensuring transparency about the money they receive from licensing and other such schemes, and how that translates to better enforcement.”

Beadle also highlighted the unfair burden placed on responsible landlords, who often bear the costs of licensing fees and other charges. He argued that the current system, where compliant landlords subsidise the enforcement against rogue operators, is unsustainable and risks driving good landlords out of the market.

The Impact on Tenants and Landlords

The failure to recover fines not only undermines enforcement efforts but also affects tenants and conscientious landlords. Tenants may continue to face substandard living conditions if rogue landlords evade penalties, whilst responsible landlords may feel penalised for adhering to regulations. The NRLA has called for a system where the “polluter pays,” ensuring that those who flout the rules bear the financial consequences.

As the government considers reforms to the private rented sector, landlords and tenants alike will be watching closely to see how enforcement mechanisms evolve. Greater transparency and accountability could help restore confidence in the system and ensure that housing standards are upheld.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Local authorities across England are ramping up inspections of privately rented properties, yet a significant portion of fines issued to rogue landlords remains unpaid. According to data obtained by the National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA), councils collected only around 25% of the penalties imposed on landlords for housing offences, despite increased enforcement efforts.

Rising Inspections, Unpaid Fines

The NRLA’s Freedom of Information request revealed that inspections under the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) rose from 85,326 in 2021/22 to 91,620 in 2023/24. However, whilst inspections have increased, councils have struggled to recover fines. Between 2023/24 and 2024/25, English councils issued nearly £30 million in penalties but recouped just £7.5 million. This disparity highlights a growing challenge in ensuring that landlords who violate housing standards are held accountable.

Calls for Greater Transparency and Enforcement

The NRLA has urged councils to publish annual reports detailing their enforcement activities in the private rented sector. This call was echoed by the cross-party Housing Select Committee, which recently recommended stronger oversight of local authority enforcement. Ben Beadle, Chief Executive of the NRLA, emphasised the need for transparency, stating: “Councils must publish annual reports ensuring transparency about the money they receive from licensing and other such schemes, and how that translates to better enforcement.”

Beadle also highlighted the unfair burden placed on responsible landlords, who often bear the costs of licensing fees and other charges. He argued that the current system, where compliant landlords subsidise the enforcement against rogue operators, is unsustainable and risks driving good landlords out of the market.

The Impact on Tenants and Landlords

The failure to recover fines not only undermines enforcement efforts but also affects tenants and conscientious landlords. Tenants may continue to face substandard living conditions if rogue landlords evade penalties, whilst responsible landlords may feel penalised for adhering to regulations. The NRLA has called for a system where the “polluter pays,” ensuring that those who flout the rules bear the financial consequences.

As the government considers reforms to the private rented sector, landlords and tenants alike will be watching closely to see how enforcement mechanisms evolve. Greater transparency and accountability could help restore confidence in the system and ensure that housing standards are upheld.

item.title: New platform targets self-managing landlords
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlords who manage their rental properties independently may find their administrative workload increasing due to evolving compliance rules and tax obligations. A newly launched platform, RentVault, aims to simplify these processes by combining property management tools into a single system.

Streamlining landlord responsibilities

RentVault offers a centralised solution for tracking key compliance documents, including gas safety certificates, EICRs and EPCs. The platform automatically monitors renewal dates, helping landlords avoid potential penalties for expired certifications. For tenancy management, it generates Section 8 notices and maintains comprehensive tenant records.

The system accommodates various property types, from single-lets to HMOs and holiday rentals, including those held through limited companies or SPVs. Landlords with overseas holdings can also manage these through the same account, potentially reducing the need for multiple management tools.

Meeting digital tax requirements

With Making Tax Digital for Income Tax coming into effect, RentVault produces quarterly financial reports formatted for major accounting software like Xero and QuickBooks. This feature could prove particularly valuable for landlords transitioning to digital record-keeping.

The platform includes an AI assistant named Iain, designed to answer landlord queries about compliance and regulations. This may help independent landlords navigate complex rule changes without needing to consult external advisors for routine matters.

RentVault offers a free tier for single-property landlords, with paid plans starting from £9.99 monthly after a 14-day trial period. For self-managing landlords juggling spreadsheets and multiple systems, such platforms could help consolidate essential tasks while adapting to regulatory changes like the Renters' Rights Act 2025 and the upcoming Private Rented Sector Database.

As compliance requirements continue evolving, digital management tools may become increasingly valuable for landlords seeking to maintain organisation whilst reducing administrative burdens. Property owners might consider comparing different platforms to find solutions that best match their portfolio size and management style.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Landlords who manage their rental properties independently may find their administrative workload increasing due to evolving compliance rules and tax obligations. A newly launched platform, RentVault, aims to simplify these processes by combining property management tools into a single system.

Streamlining landlord responsibilities

RentVault offers a centralised solution for tracking key compliance documents, including gas safety certificates, EICRs and EPCs. The platform automatically monitors renewal dates, helping landlords avoid potential penalties for expired certifications. For tenancy management, it generates Section 8 notices and maintains comprehensive tenant records.

The system accommodates various property types, from single-lets to HMOs and holiday rentals, including those held through limited companies or SPVs. Landlords with overseas holdings can also manage these through the same account, potentially reducing the need for multiple management tools.

Meeting digital tax requirements

With Making Tax Digital for Income Tax coming into effect, RentVault produces quarterly financial reports formatted for major accounting software like Xero and QuickBooks. This feature could prove particularly valuable for landlords transitioning to digital record-keeping.

The platform includes an AI assistant named Iain, designed to answer landlord queries about compliance and regulations. This may help independent landlords navigate complex rule changes without needing to consult external advisors for routine matters.

RentVault offers a free tier for single-property landlords, with paid plans starting from £9.99 monthly after a 14-day trial period. For self-managing landlords juggling spreadsheets and multiple systems, such platforms could help consolidate essential tasks while adapting to regulatory changes like the Renters' Rights Act 2025 and the upcoming Private Rented Sector Database.

As compliance requirements continue evolving, digital management tools may become increasingly valuable for landlords seeking to maintain organisation whilst reducing administrative burdens. Property owners might consider comparing different platforms to find solutions that best match their portfolio size and management style.
